summary: Gruber eviscerates a Bloomberg opinion column by Leonid Bershidsky that characterized WhatsApp's end-to-end encryption as a 'gimmick' or 'pointless' after a remote code execution vulnerability was exploited by NSO Group. Gruber argues that Bershidsky commits a fundamental error by treating security as binary rather than as a continuum. He explains that while the WhatsApp exploit was serious, it required world-class hacking talent and does not invalidate the enormous value of E2E encryption, which protects against far more common threats like subpoenas and network snooping. Gruber also tracks Bloomberg's multiple headline revisions as evidence of editorial incompetence, and ties this to Bloomberg's lingering credibility problem from the unretracted 'Big Hack' story. The detailed footnotes explain why the distinction between E2E and non-E2E encryption genuinely matters for users.
key_points: Security is a continuum, not binary — the WhatsApp exploit doesn't make E2E encryption worthless, just as a picked lock doesn't make locks pointless
Bloomberg has cycled through multiple increasingly less absurd headlines for the piece, trackable via URL slugs and title tags, yet still hasn't retracted it
Exploiting a remote code execution vulnerability requires world-class talent, while obtaining unencrypted communications via subpoena requires only routine legal process
Bloomberg's cybersecurity credibility was already in tatters from the unretracted 'Big Hack' story, making this editorial failure worse
E2E encryption eliminates the middleman server as a target for attack or subpoena, which is a genuinely important security distinction from non-E2E encryption
money_quotes: End-to-end encryption is not a panacea.
It's like if you have a secure communication line between two rooms, but an attacker gains entry into one of the rooms. The problem is not with the communication line.
Security is not binary, though — which is obvious if you give it even a moment's thought. A locked door is more secure than an unlocked one.
Obtaining a subpoena requires nothing of the sort — simply the regular mechanics of law enforcement, judicial oversight, and compliance with the law.

Obtaining email via subpoena requires you to be able to make a free throw; doing what this week's WhatsApp exploit seemingly accomplished requires you to be Steph Curry and hit 9 three-pointers in a single game against a playoff-caliber NBA defense.
Complete guaranteed security with well-known exceptions. It boggles the mind that this was written and edited by sentient humans.
These various evolutions on the headline range from bad to criminally bad.
key_insight: Security exists on a continuum, and dismissing end-to-end encryption because a world-class exploit bypassed it is like calling door locks pointless because a master lockpick exists.
